--- SOURCE 1: Animal Welfare Implications of Digital Tools for Monitoring ... ---
URL: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/

SUMMARY:
However, the knowledge of the potential effects on animal welfare of digital technology for monitoring and managing grazing livestock is limited, especially regarding drones and virtual fences. Keywords: animal welfare, cattle, monitoring, precision livestock farming, sensor, sheep, virtual fence This review includes the use of digital technology and new tools for the management of animals, including animal welfare monitoring with the help of drones and virtual fences, as these include sensors and automated responses. 126.Anderson D.M., Estell R.E., Holechek J.L., Ivey S., Smith G.B. Virtual herding for flexible livestock management–a review. 142.Lee C., Colditz I.G., Campbell D.L.M. A framework to assess the impact of new animal management technologies on welfare: A case study of virtual fencing.

Animal Welfare Implications of Digital Tools for Monitoring and Management of Cattle and Sheep on Pasture

[PMC Copyright notice](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/about/copyright/)

PMCID: PMC8000582 PMID: [33804235](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33804235/)

Abstract
--------

### Simple Summary

Monitoring the welfare of cattle and sheep in large pastures can be time-consuming, especially if the animals are scattered over large areas in semi-natural pastures. There are several technologies for monitoring animals with wearable or remote equipment for recording physiological or behavioural parameters and trigger alarms when the acquired information deviates from the normal. Automatic equipment allows continuous monitoring and may give more information than manual monitoring. Ear tags with electronic identification can detect visits to specific points. Collars with positioning (GPS) units can assess the animals’ movements and habitat selection and, to some extent, their health and welfare. Digitally determined virtual fences, instead of the traditional physical ones, have the potential to keep livestock within a predefined area using audio signals in combination with weak electric shocks, although some individuals may have difficulties in responding as intended, potentially resulting in reduced animal welfare. Remote technology such as drones equipped with cameras can be used to count animals, determine their position and study their behaviour. Drones can also herd and move animals. However, the knowledge of the potential effects on animal welfare of digital technology for monitoring and managing grazing livestock is limited, especially regarding drones and virtual fences.

### Abstract

The opportunities for natural animal behaviours in pastures imply animal welfare benefits. Nevertheless, monitoring the animals can be challenging. The use of sensors, cameras, positioning equipment and unmanned aerial vehicles in large pastures has the potential to improve animal welfare surveillance. Directly or indirectly, sensors measure environmental factors together with the behaviour and physiological state of the animal, and deviations can trigger alarms for, e.g., disease, heat stress and imminent calving. Electronic positioning includes Radio Frequency Identification (RFID) for the recording of animals at fixed points. Positioning units (GPS) mounted on collars can determine animal movements over large areas, determine their habitat and, somewhat, health and welfare. In combination with other sensors, such units can give information that helps to evaluate the welfare of free-ranging animals. Drones equipped with cameras can also locate and count the animals, as well as herd them. Digitally defined virtual fences can keep animals within a predefined area without the use of physical barriers, relying on acoustic signals and weak electric shocks. Due to individual variations in learning ability, some individuals may be exposed to numerous electric shocks, which might compromise their welfare. More research and development are required, especially regarding the use of drones and virtual fences.

**Keywords:** animal welfare, cattle, monitoring, precision livestock farming, sensor, sheep, virtual fence

1. Introduction
---------------

In modern livestock production systems, animals are largely kept indoors or in small enclosures. Pasture-based production systems are decreasing as the demands for high profitability increase. However, pasture-based systems usually offer better hygiene than indoors, provide animals with a softer surface than the commonly used concrete in buildings and allows them to perform natural behaviours, without serious restrictions on their movements. Such conditions have many positive animal welfare effects that meet common consumer expectations [[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B1-animals-11-00829)]. In addition, grazing can be beneficial for biodiversity [[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B2-animals-11-00829)], soil preservation [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B3-animals-11-00829)] and carbon sequestration [[4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B4-animals-11-00829)]. It is good farming practice to regularly inspect the animals’ health and welfare in a pasture, which is also regulated by law in some countries. In Sweden, for example, animals should be inspected at least once daily.

The automated inspection or monitoring from a distance by the use of digital technologies might reduce the labour substantially. In fact, it has been suggested that such technologies would allow farmers to monitor and manage animals more intensively than otherwise, resulting in a higher efficiency in production, lower environmental impact and improved animal welfare [[5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B5-animals-11-00829)]. Technological developments in the last two decades have led to several digital applications in the livestock sector. Since environmental conditions outdoors vary in terms of weather, climate, physical conditions, vegetation and geography, the transfer of data over long distances can be technically challenging. Most of the existing sensor technology in agriculture was developed for indoor use. New developments of remotely controlled unmanned aerial vehicles (UAV), commonly called drones, have further increased the possibilities for farm animal monitoring and management.

Sensors record more or less continuously around the clock, and the information acquired can be much more detailed than a single manual inspection per day. The use of sensors thus might have the potential to promote grazing in large pasture areas with an increased monitoring of factors indicating the animals’ welfare status. Nevertheless, sensors can generate objective information about events in the environment or the animals’ conditions (activity, physiology, etc.), but human judgement is often needed to interpret the data, and relevant actions are required to secure the animals’ welfare. An essential question is therefore whether, and to what extent, digital technology can replace manual inspections. A combination of automated digital monitoring and manual follow-up inspections may prove to be a reasonable compromise. Moreover, some applications of wearables and digital technologies may also influence the individual animals in a way that can compromise their welfare.

This systematic review addresses the use of digital technologies for health and welfare monitoring and the management of livestock kept in large pasture areas. The review is mainly focused on cattle (_Bos taurus_) and domestic sheep (_Ovis aries_), but most of the information is applicable to other livestock species as well. This review includes the use of digital technology and new tools for the management of animals, including animal welfare monitoring with the help of drones and virtual fences, as these include sensors and automated responses.

A systematic literature search in English and German was performed in Web of Science Core Collection, CAB Abstracts® and Scopus from all available publication years. The following search string was used: (cattle* OR bovin* OR beef* OR *cow* OR *calf* OR *calves OR *sheep* OR ewe* OR ovine OR lamb* OR *rind* OR *kuh* OR *kalb* OR *kälber* OR *schaf* OR *lamm*) AND (outdoor* OR rangeland* OR enclosure* OR pasture* OR graz* freerange* OR free-range OR “free range” OR *draussen* OR gehege* OR weide* OR freiland*) AND (“new techni” OR “inventive techni” OR infrared* OR drone* OR *positioning* OR gps* OR “invisible fenc” OR “wireless fenc” OR “virtual fenc” OR *accelero* OR *logger* OR *thermograph* OR *telemetr* OR sensor OR sensors OR “neue techni” OR “erfinderische techni” OR *infrarot* OR *drohne* OR “virtueller zaun”) AND (“precision livestock” OR *vigilanc* OR *monitor* OR *surveill* OR control* OR contain* OR *präzisionsviehhaltung* OR *wachsamkeit* OR *überwachung* OR *positionierung* OR “unsichtbarer zaun” OR “unsichtbare zäune” OR *kontroll* OR *eindämm*). The search resulted in 532 references after duplicate removal. From these, relevant references were chosen to be used for the review. They were supplemented with references already known to the authors, resulting in 148 references in total.

2. Use of Digital Technologies in Livestock Production
------------------------------------------------------

### 2.1. Precision Livestock Farming

The use of sensor technologies in livestock production, often called Precision Livestock Farming (PLF), is a relatively new phenomenon starting with the development of the milking robot in the 1990s. PLF involves modern information technology (IT) in order to provide farmers with information for the control of the production process. PLF is generally defined as a management system that offers continuous, automatic monitoring and control of animal behaviour, health, welfare, production and reproduction, as well as environmental impact of the production, in real time [[6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B6-animals-11-00829),[7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B7-animals-11-00829)]. By continuous monitoring of the animals, the farmer can immediately detect changes that may indicate that something is wrong [[6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B6-animals-11-00829),[8](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B8-animals-11-00829)]. PLF has the potential to completely or partially replace direct inspections on-site once or several times a day.

In recent years, technological advances have made it feasible to receive accurate data from affordable sensors and instruments, including cameras, microphones, accelerometers (sometimes combined with a gyroscope), thermometers, conductivity meters and devices to determine the physical location. The data are transmitted to computers, where they can be processed and interpreted into useful information, such as identifying animals not following an expected pattern ([Figure 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#animals-11-00829-f001)). The Internet of Things (IoT) solutions and several other technologies can be used for wireless communication, data processing and storage. The information obtained can be used to guide some form of automatic or manual action.

#### Figure 1.

[![Image 9: Figure 1](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/6871/8000582/45a4940fb870/animals-11-00829-g001.jpg)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=8000582_animals-11-00829-g001.jpg)

[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/figure/animals-11-00829-f001/)

Model of Precision Livestock Farming showing the principal flow of data, the process control where real-time results are compared with a target value, followed by a control function that determines if the information from the sensor matches the target value [[6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B6-animals-11-00829)].

### 2.2. Accuracy and Reliability of PLF Systems

The accuracy of a PLF system, i.e., if the abnormalities in animal behaviour, physiology and health are detected correctly, is assessed by several methods depending on the technology involved. The positive predictive value (PPV) is the probability that a positive system output or alarm corresponds to a true state or event. Conversely, the sensitivity (Se) is the probability that a true state or event is followed by a positive system output or alarm, while the specificity (Sp) is the probability that the absence of a true state or event does not produce a positive system output or alarm. Measures to increase the sensitivity of the system by lowering the alarm threshold necessarily can lead to more false positive outputs due to the lowered specificity. The relationship between sensitivity and specificity for different threshold values can be visualised with a Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve, and the ability of the system to combine a high sensitivity with a high specificity can be calculated as the area under the ROC curve [[9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B9-animals-11-00829)]. Other common statistical quality measures are the Pearson or Spearman correlation coefficients (_r_ and _ρ_, respectively), the concordance correlation coefficient (CCC), which expresses the degree of linear relationship between two continuous quantities (actual state and sensor signal), and the coefficient of determination (R 2), which is the proportion of variation of a studied quantity (the actual state) that can be explained by one or more other variables (the sensor signals). The resolution (i.e., the degree of perceptible detail) of a camera system can be expressed as the size of the minimum distinguishable object in the terrain. Furthermore, in an image analysis, the term “hit rate” is used to describe the ability to accurately recognise objects in images.

So far, there is no general agreement or accepted methodology to statistically validate the different sensors or PLF systems, nor to determine what is required for a sensor or system to be considered validated, which makes comparisons of different sensor systems difficult. The introduction of standardised validation protocols would therefore be helpful [[10](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B10-animals-11-00829)]. Guidelines for evaluating sensor technologies are currently being prepared by the International Committee for Animal Recording [[11](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B11-animals-11-00829)].

Reliability is not only about correct measurements. There is a risk that advanced technology for animal supervision could completely cease to function, which would also compromise the reliability. We have not found any systematic information on the practical operational reliability of the various commercially available systems for monitoring farm animals, nor have we found information about the presence of alarms for technical faults, although such events occur occasionally.

3. Sensor Technologies
----------------------

A sensor detects conditions or events in the environment or the animal, for example, by measuring the physical, physiological or behavioural indicators or changes in animal management (feeding, bedding and milking), and can provide information about the animal’s condition, e.g., its health, to help the caretaker assess the need for corrective action [[12](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B12-animals-11-00829),[13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B13-animals-11-00829)].

Sensors can be animal-based or non-animal-based. Animal-based sensors are attached to the animal on ear tags, collars, leg straps or internal boluses or implants. In contrast, non-animal-based sensors are located in the animal’s vicinity—for example, in cameras or automatic scales. In dairy production, there is a variety of commercially available sensors that can be used for animal health and welfare monitoring. They are usually developed for use in indoor systems, but the development of new sensor technologies and infrastructure for data transfer enables their gradual application in grazing animals ([Table 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#animals-11-00829-t001)).

### Table 1.

Examples of the sensors used in dairy farming, what they measure and what the alarm signal can inform the stockperson [[14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B14-animals-11-00829)].

| Type of Sensor | Measurement | Information |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Activity | Activity, rumination, lying time, step count | Oestrus, calving, lameness, general health |
| pH sensor | Rumen pH | Rumen acidosis |
| Camera | Activity, feed intake, body shape | Ketosis, body condition, lameness, mastitis |
| Thermometer, thermography | Body temperature thermal body surface radiation | Water intake, calving, infection, lameness, general health |
| Microphone | Rumination time | Rumen function, general health, oestrus, calving |

[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/table/animals-11-00829-t001/)

### 3.1. Sensors to Measure Physiology

Body temperature can be monitored in order to identify health disorders, changes associated with reproduction, heat stress and hypothermia. There are a number of commercial body temperature sensors, where the most common ones are attached to an ear tag or given in the form of a bolus. Changes in the ambient temperature and the incorrect attachment of ear tags tend to reduce their reliability [[15](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B15-animals-11-00829)].

Feed and water intake, as well as changes in the rumen microflora, can be detected by rumen boluses. Temperature changes that occur when the animal drinks can be used to calculate their daily water intake and detect if the animal has not been drinking for a certain period of time [[16](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B16-animals-11-00829)]. Commercially available rumen boluses (a sensor built into a container administered to the rumen) with pH sensors can be used to monitor the condition of the rumen and health disorders leading to a decrease in pH [[17](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B17-animals-11-00829)], often associated with inadequate feeding routines. In addition, changes in pasture access and composition can be detected if they affect the reticuloruminal pH [[18](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B18-animals-11-00829)]. Several studies have detected subacute rumen acidosis using commercially available boluses that measure the pH and temperature [[19](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B19-animals-11-00829),[20](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B20-animals-11-00829)].

Implants have been shown to accurately record heart rates in free-grazing sheep [[21](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B21-animals-11-00829)]. However, for a wider agricultural use, real-time data transfer needs to be developed for this method to become commercially applicable.

### 3.2. Sensors to Measure Behaviour

Changes in behaviour, such as activity, feed intake or rumination, can be linked to normal conditions such as heat and preparations for calving but, also, to disease and pain. A change in animal behaviour may not always be linked to a particular condition but may indicate the need for group management. The continuous monitoring of behaviours makes it possible to identify not only diseased animals but, also, animals at risk of developing health disorders [[22](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B22-animals-11-00829)]. Commercially available accelerometers, mainly 3D, are usually attached to neck collars, leg straps or ear tags but can also be incorporated into boluses. Depending on where the sensor is located, different behaviours can be related to the accelerometer signals, and thus, the behaviours be quantified. Sensors attached around the neck or in the ear of the animal often provide information on the activity alone, i.e., the total amount of movement [[23](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B23-animals-11-00829)]. Sensors attached around the animal’s legs can also provide information on the number of steps, lying and standing times and the number of lying bouts. There are a number of techniques for measuring rumination and feeding behaviours, of which the most common are 3D accelerometers, pressure gauges and microphones [[24](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B24-animals-11-00829),[25](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B25-animals-11-00829),[26](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B26-animals-11-00829),[27](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B27-animals-11-00829),[28](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B28-animals-11-00829)]. Commercially available systems mainly use 3D accelerometers and microphones. Depending on the system, they can provide information on the rumination time and number of chewing movements, as well as feed and water intakes [[29](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B29-animals-11-00829)]. Duncan and Meyer [[30](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B30-animals-11-00829)] showed that 3D accelerometers can be used to indicate that calving has begun.

Sensor systems can detect a variety of infectious diseases, metabolic disorders and lameness. For example, sensors have been shown to detect changes in the locomotion and lying and/or feeding behaviours in cattle or sheep affected by mastitis [[31](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B31-animals-11-00829),[32](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B32-animals-11-00829),[33](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B33-animals-11-00829)], metritis [[34](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B34-animals-11-00829)], ketosis [[35](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B35-animals-11-00829)], lameness [[36](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B36-animals-11-00829),[37](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B37-animals-11-00829),[38](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B38-animals-11-00829),[39](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B39-animals-11-00829)], respiratory disease [[40](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B40-animals-11-00829)], gastrointestinal parasites [[41](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B41-animals-11-00829),[42](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B42-animals-11-00829),[43](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B43-animals-11-00829),[44](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B44-animals-11-00829),[45](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B45-animals-11-00829)] or a clinical disease in general [[46](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#B46-animals-11-00829)]. Several of the conditions can also be detected at an earlier stage than by manual monitoring, even before the onset of clinical signs.

The major obstacles to a more general use of sensor-aided monitoring in large and remote pastures are the limitations in data transmission and energy supplies. A few systems marketed for use in large pastures have been validated scientifically ([Table 2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8000582/#animals-11-00829-t002)). It is worth noting that these systems were primarily designed for use in housed dairy herds.

#### Table 2.

Commercially available and scientifically validated animal-based sensors for monitoring ruminant behaviours in pastures. 1 _ρ_ = Spearman’s correlation coefficient, _r_ = Pearson’s correlation coefficient, CCC = concordance correlation coefficient, Se = sensitivity, Sp =

--- SOURCE 2: Technology's Impact on Farm Animal Welfare ---
URL: https://www.rspcaassured.org.uk/news-and-updates/posts/technology-and-farmed-animal-welfare/

SUMMARY:
New technologies have had a positive impact on how farmed animals are cared for and raised, for example, animals now benefit from improved health monitoring.

FULL CONTENT:
![RSPCA Assured Logo](/media/yjnpiozz/rspca-assured-logo-coloured-1.png)

# The effects of technology on farmed animal welfare

Technology has changed almost every aspect of humans’ lives, and the same is true for farmed animals. New technologies have had a positive impact on how farmed animals are cared for and raised, for example, animals now benefit from improved health monitoring. However, technology can also present challenges for animal welfare.

We spoke to Beth, a scientific officer in the RSPCA’s farmed animal department, to find out more about the benefits, and drawbacks, of technology in the farming industry.

![](/media/aall0jba/broilers-outside.jpg?center=0.646811769073696,0.45808227507052685&mode=crop&width=120&height=63&rnd=133759863096370000&quality=75)

## Technology that benefits animal welfare

“The RSPCA welcomes new technologies which improve animal health and welfare and help give farmed animals a better life. We've seen fantastic examples of how artificial intelligence (AI) can precisely detect health or welfare concerns in animals. This allows earlier treatment or even the prevention of health problems.”

“For instance, the investment in AI technology on salmon farms can continuously identify thousands of individuals, reduce the handling of fish which causes stress, and alert producers to issues that can’t easily be detected by the human eye.”

## Welfare impact of maximising production

However, while the effects of some technologies may improve animal welfare, the RSPCA also has concerns that some technologies focus on increased productivity and that this is often prioritised over the welfare of the animals.

“The pressure to feed a growing global population and increasing demands for animal protein means that the majority of the farm industry is focused on maximising production. Farming has become more ‘intensive’ to produce more meat, eggs and dairy, faster and at a lower cost.”

![](/media/mgvmsvtd/broilers.jpg?center=0.62978619013101778,0.35153277756026213&mode=crop&width=8&height=6&rnd=133759863462930000&quality=75)

“While there’s no universally agreed definition of ‘intensive farming', generally very intensive farming involves close confinement systems - for example, cages and crates - more animals kept together with low space allowances, barren environments - and genetic selection for high productivity at the expense of animal health. In other words, lower-welfare farming.”

“Animals that are confined in cages or crates and kept in barren environments with nothing to do can become very frustrated and stressed. Cages are not permitted on RSPCA Assured farms,  and animals must always have environmental enrichment items to explore, such as straw for pigs to root in and pecking objects for poultry. They must also have plenty of space to freely move around.”

## Machines shouldn’t replace human care

The RSPCA is also concerned about technology being used in lower-welfare farming so more animals can be kept together in smaller spaces, fed automatically to maximise weight gain and monitored for health problems. Issues can arise where humans rely too much on technology, where animal behaviour changes may go unnoticed or when the systems fail, which could result in the animals not receiving adequate care.

“When technology is focused on making animals more productive, it can mean they may need to cope with worse conditions. For instance, feed intake is maximised for faster growth, but this is more food than the animal would naturally consume.”

“Other technologies which require the animal to 'learn' something, such as virtual fencing and automatic feeders, may cause stress to animals. Those who do not learn quickly may be negatively impacted by not being fed enough, or receiving repeated electric shocks from fence lines.”

“Animals can’t be cared for by machines. For this reason, the RSPCA welfare standards, which must be met by all RSPCA Assured members, state they always need to be looked after by a well-trained stockperson who understands animal behaviour and welfare.”

## Implications of breeding technologies

Technology can also be used to genetically select animals for certain traits. Some are bred to make them grow faster, gain weight more quickly and produce more of the type of meat required, for example, more breast meat in chickens.

With an estimated one billion chickens farmed in the UK alone each year, more than 90% of them are ‘fast-growing’ breeds that have been genetically selected to grow rapidly to reach slaughter weight as quickly as possible. Their legs cannot develop fast enough to support their body weight, so in their final days, they can struggle to move.

“Only slower growing, higher welfare breeds are permitted on the RSPCA Assured scheme. This means they are able to build muscle and their bones can develop to support their body weight - so they can move freely, explore their environment and exhibit their natural behaviours.”

## How can you help?

If you eat meat, fish, eggs or dairy products and would like to help ensure farmed animals live better lives, look out for RSPCA Assured-labelled products when you’re shopping.

You can also take action by [lobbying your supermarket](/retailers/lobby-your-supermarket/) to only sell higher welfare produce.

13 Nov 2024

![website manager](/media/lu0f4pbk/jodie01.jpg?anchor=center&mode=crop&width=8&height=8&rnd=133923840910800000&quality=75)

--- SOURCE 3: What Role Does Technology Play in Animal Welfare? ---
URL: https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/question/what-role-does-technology-play-in-animal-welfare/

SUMMARY:
Benefits of Technology in Monitoring Animal Welfare · Real-time data provides up-to-the-minute insights into animal behavior and health.

FULL CONTENT:
![Lifestyle → Sustainability Directory](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/Lifestyle-Sustainability-Directory-Logotype.svg)
![Lifestyle → Sustainability Directory](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/Lifestyle-Sustainability-Directory-Logotype.svg)
![What Role Does Technology Play in Animal Welfare?](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/what-role-does-technology-play-in-animal-welfare-question-image.webp)

# What Role Does Technology Play in Animal Welfare?

![A detailed macro view reveals a microchip covered in water droplets, a potent symbol for sustainable technology. This image subtly highlights the environmental impact of electronics production and the crucial need for digital responsibility. It urges reflection on water conservation in manufacturing processes and advocates for resource efficiency and electronic waste reduction in the technology sector.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-technology-a-microchip-adorned-with-water-droplets-symbolizing.jpg "Sustainable Technology a Microchip Adorned with Water Droplets Symbolizing Digital Responsibility and Environmental Consciousness")

## Fundamentals

Technology’s presence in our lives is undeniable, and its influence extends far beyond smartphones and social media. Consider its role in safeguarding the well-being of animals. From tracking [endangered species Meaning → Species at risk of extinction, reflecting ecosystem distress and human impact.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/term/endangered-species/)  to monitoring livestock health, technology offers solutions to challenges that once seemed insurmountable.

At its core, [animal welfare Meaning → Animal welfare, within the framework of sustainability, denotes the ethical responsibility humans have to ensure the well-being of animals, both domestic and wild.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/area/animal-welfare/)  revolves around ensuring animals have a good quality of life, free from unnecessary suffering. This encompasses physical health, mental well-being, and the ability to express natural behaviors. Traditional approaches to animal welfare often rely on visual observation and manual intervention, which can be time-consuming and limited in scope. Technology offers a transformative upgrade, enabling us to gather more comprehensive data and implement more proactive strategies.

One of the most significant contributions of technology is in the realm of **monitoring**. Imagine sensors attached to wild animals, providing real-time data on their location, movement patterns, and even physiological indicators like heart rate and body temperature. This information can be invaluable for conservation efforts, allowing researchers to track populations, understand habitat use, and identify potential threats.

For livestock, technology offers a path towards more humane farming practices. Automated feeding systems can ensure that animals receive the right amount of nutrition at the right time, while environmental sensors can monitor temperature, humidity, and air quality to create optimal living conditions. These technologies not only benefit animal welfare but can also improve efficiency and productivity for farmers.

Beyond monitoring, technology also plays a role in **diagnosis and treatment**. Advanced imaging techniques like MRI and CT scans can help veterinarians diagnose illnesses and injuries more accurately, while robotic surgery offers minimally invasive options for complex procedures. Telemedicine is also expanding access to veterinary care, particularly in remote areas where specialists may be scarce.

Even seemingly simple technologies like **mobile apps** can make a difference. There are apps that help pet owners track their animal’s activity levels, manage their diet, and even connect with local veterinarians. These tools empower individuals to take a more active role in their animal’s health and well-being.

It’s important to acknowledge that technology is not a panacea. It’s a tool, and like any tool, it can be used for good or ill. Concerns have been raised about the potential for technology to be used to exploit animals, for example, through intensive farming practices or the creation of artificial environments that restrict natural behaviors. The ethical implications of technology in animal welfare must be carefully considered, ensuring that its use aligns with principles of compassion and respect.

As technology continues to evolve, its role in animal welfare will undoubtedly expand. The challenge lies in harnessing its potential to create a more humane and sustainable [future Meaning → Exploring future scenarios and possibilities is the core focus of the Prism sustainability satellite.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/)  for all living beings.

![Delicate water droplets sit atop a circuit board, symbolizing the fragility of technology and the urgency for sustainable solutions in our digital lifestyle. This image evokes themes of eco-conscious living and emphasizes the environmental impact of electronics. It is a visual reminder of the need for resource efficiency and responsible consumption within our tech-driven world.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/water-droplets-on-circuit-board-emphasizing-fragile-electronics-and-sustainable.jpg "Water Droplets on Circuit Board Emphasizing Fragile Electronics and Sustainable Technology Concerns for Green Lifestyle")

### Benefits of Technology in Monitoring Animal Welfare

![A song sparrow with intricate feather details perches on a weathered wooden post, exemplifying natural habitat within an accessible view. This image promotes biodiversity and habitat conservation, key tenets of a sustainable life. It encourages green living and environmental awareness, showcasing urban wildlife as a vital part of our ecological balance.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/observing-intricate-feather-details-of-song-sparrow-perched-on-weathered-wood.jpg "Observing Intricate Feather Details of Song Sparrow Perched on Weathered Wood Embodying Natural Habitat Conservation and Sustainable Lifestyle Principles")

### Ethical Considerations of Technology in Animal Welfare

![A gentle bird protectively watches over speckled eggs in its natural nest, embodying sustainable life and environmental care. Soft feathers and leaves add to the organic composition, highlighting the delicate balance of nature and promoting earth-friendly living. This serene scene emphasizes conservation and biodiversity, reminding us of the pure beauty of our ecosystems and the need for minimal impact actions to ensure a wholesome, regenerative planet.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/gentle-bird-protecting-natural-eggs-a-study-in-sustainable-life-and.jpg "Gentle Bird Protecting Natural Eggs a Study in Sustainable Life and Environmental Harmony")

## Intermediate

Stepping beyond the fundamentals, we find that technology’s influence on animal welfare is deeply interwoven with our evolving understanding of animal cognition, behavior, and their intrinsic value within ecosystems. The intersection of these factors presents both opportunities and responsibilities for leveraging technology responsibly.

Consider the growing field of **precision livestock farming**. This approach uses sensors, data analytics, and automation to manage livestock with greater individual attention. Rather than treating animals as a homogenous group, [precision livestock farming Meaning ∞ The application of technology to continuously monitor individual animals to optimize their health, welfare, and productivity within a sustainable framework.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/term/precision-livestock-farming/)  recognizes that each animal has unique needs and preferences. Sensors can monitor individual feeding habits, activity levels, and social interactions, allowing farmers to tailor their management practices to optimize welfare.

For example, wearable sensors on dairy cows can detect subtle changes in gait or posture that may indicate lameness. Early detection allows farmers to intervene before the condition becomes severe, reducing pain and suffering. Similarly, automated milking systems can adjust pressure and duration based on individual cow characteristics, minimizing discomfort and improving milk yield. Precision livestock farming represents a shift towards a more individualized and responsive approach to animal care.

Technology is also revolutionizing **wildlife conservation**. Drones equipped with thermal imaging cameras can be used to detect poachers, monitor endangered species, and assess habitat damage. Acoustic monitoring devices can record animal vocalizations, providing valuable insights into population dynamics and behavior patterns. Geographic information systems (GIS) can be used to map wildlife corridors, identify areas of conflict between humans and animals, and plan conservation strategies.

The use of **artificial intelligence (AI)** in animal welfare is also gaining traction. AI algorithms can analyze vast amounts of data from sensors, cameras, and other sources to identify patterns and predict potential problems. For example, AI can be used to detect signs of stress or illness in livestock, alert farmers to potential welfare concerns, and even suggest interventions. In wildlife conservation, AI can be used to identify individual animals from camera trap images, track their movements, and predict their risk of poaching.

However, the application of AI in animal welfare raises ethical questions about **data bias** and **algorithmic transparency**. If the data used to train AI algorithms is biased towards certain breeds or management practices, the resulting algorithms may perpetuate existing inequalities. It is crucial to ensure that AI systems are developed and used in a way that is fair, equitable, and transparent.

Beyond the practical applications, technology also plays a role in **changing human attitudes towards animals**. Virtual reality (VR) experiences can allow people to experience the world from an animal’s perspective, fostering empathy and understanding. Social media platforms can be used to raise awareness about animal welfare issues and mobilize support for conservation efforts. Technology can be a powerful tool for promoting a more compassionate and responsible relationship between humans and animals.

Ultimately, the effectiveness of technology in promoting animal welfare depends on how it is used and the values that guide its application. A focus on animal sentience, respect for natural behaviors, and a commitment to minimizing suffering are essential for ensuring that technology serves the best interests of animals.

Moving forward, a holistic approach that integrates technological innovation with [ethical considerations Meaning → Ethical considerations are the principles guiding our choices to ensure fairness, justice, and well-being for all, now and in the future.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/term/ethical-considerations/)  and a deep understanding of animal needs will be essential for advancing animal welfare in a sustainable and meaningful way.

![A close up image displays a sustainable electronic component with water droplets, highlighting eco conscious technology and a connection to natural elements. The textured material and water beads emphasize green computing and digital sustainability for responsible consumption. This earth friendly gadget represents resource efficiency, product longevity, and minimalist living in a digital world.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/water-droplets-on-eco-friendly-electronic-component-showcasing-sustainable.jpg "Water Droplets on Eco Friendly Electronic Component Showcasing Sustainable Technology and Natural Elements")

### Precision Livestock Farming Techniques

| Technique | Description | Benefit to Animal Welfare |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Wearable Sensors | Monitor activity, health metrics | Early detection of illness, injury |
| Automated Feeding | Individualized nutrition | Optimized health and growth |
| Environmental Sensors | Monitor temperature, humidity | Improved living conditions |

![A close-up depicts electronic waste and component decay, highlighting the environmental impact of discarded technology. This image emphasizes the need for sustainable technology, responsible consumption, and improved e-waste management to lessen pollution and promote resource conservation and a circular economy. The grainy substance covering the circuit board underscores the degradation of electronic devices and the urgency of eco conscious digital lifestyles.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/exploring-electronic-waste-decay-a-micro-view-into-sustainable-technology-and.jpg "Exploring Electronic Waste Decay a Micro View into Sustainable Technology and Environmental Pollution Impact")

### Applications of AI in Animal Welfare

![Captured from directly above, a textured wooden base edged with salt supports a smaller handmade soap similarly embellished, presenting an image of sustainable self care and holistic wellness. This eco conscious setup showcases a dedication to a low impact lifestyle and a minimal approach. The image embodies simplicity and natural skincare as core components of green living.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/eco-conscious-living-natural-salt-scrub-bar-minimalist-sustainable-home-spa.jpg "Eco Conscious Living Natural Salt Scrub Bar Minimalist Sustainable Home Spa Experience for Green Living Wellness")

## Academic

The discourse surrounding technology’s role in animal welfare extends beyond mere practical application, entering a complex realm where philosophical underpinnings of human-animal relationships intersect with socio-economic realities and the potential for both utopian advancements and dystopian regressions. A critical analysis requires situating technological interventions within broader frameworks of ethical theory, ecological sustainability, and the evolving landscape of animal rights.

Drawing upon the works of scholars like **Peter Singer** and **Tom Regan**, we can frame the discussion around the concept of [animal sentience Meaning → Animal sentience denotes the capacity of animals to experience feelings and sensations, including pain, pleasure, fear, and joy.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/area/animal-sentience/)  and the moral obligations it entails. Singer’s utilitarian perspective emphasizes the minimization of suffering, suggesting that technology should be used to reduce pain and improve the overall well-being of animals. Regan’s rights-based approach, on the other hand, argues that animals have inherent value and are entitled to certain fundamental rights, regardless of their utility to humans. This perspective raises questions about the ethical implications of using technology to manipulate animal behavior or confine them in artificial environments, even if it ostensibly improves their welfare.

The application of technology in animal agriculture is particularly contentious. While proponents argue that precision livestock farming can improve efficiency and reduce environmental impact, critics raise concerns about the intensification of farming practices and the commodification of animals. The use of genetic engineering to enhance productivity or disease resistance also sparks debate, with some arguing that it violates the integrity of animal species and undermines their natural capacity to adapt to changing environments. Furthermore, the concentration of data and control in the hands of large corporations raises concerns about [power Meaning → Power is the rate at which energy is generated and consumed, with a focus on transitioning to clean and renewable sources.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/)  imbalances and the potential for exploitation.

**Biosemiotics** offers another lens through which to examine technology’s influence on animal welfare. This field explores how animals perceive and interact with their environment, highlighting the importance of sensory experiences and communication in their overall well-being. Technology can be used to enhance animal environments by providing opportunities for exploration, play, and social interaction.

However, it can also disrupt natural communication patterns and create sensory overload, leading to stress and behavioral problems. Understanding the semiotic dimensions of animal experience is crucial for designing technologies that truly promote their welfare.

The ecological implications of technology in animal welfare must also be considered. While technology can be used to monitor and protect endangered species, it can also contribute to habitat destruction and environmental degradation. The production of electronic devices requires vast amounts of resources and energy, and the disposal of e-waste can pollute ecosystems and harm wildlife. A sustainable approach to animal welfare requires minimizing the environmental footprint of technological interventions and promoting practices that protect biodiversity and ecological integrity.

A truly ethical and sustainable approach to animal welfare requires a paradigm shift in our relationship with animals. Instead of viewing them as resources to be exploited or objects to be manipulated, we must recognize them as sentient beings with inherent value and respect their right to live a life that is consistent with their natural capacities and needs. Technology can play a role in this transformation by empowering us to understand animals better, treat them more compassionately, and protect their habitats. However, it is essential to ensure that technology serves as a tool for liberation, rather than a means of further entrenching systems of oppression and exploitation.

Ultimately, the question of what role technology plays in animal welfare is not simply a matter of technological feasibility or economic efficiency. It is a question of values, ethics, and our vision for a more just and sustainable world. As we move forward, we must prioritize animal sentience, ecological integrity, and social justice, ensuring that technology serves as a force for positive change in the lives of all living beings.

![In soft natural light, a light orange tabby cat rests on a textured concrete surface, showcasing mindful composure. This domestic pet embodies simple living and an eco-friendly approach to sustainable pet companionship. Its relaxed posture and gentle gaze in the outdoor setting reflect responsible animal care and the inherent wellbeing found in a green lifestyle.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/gentle-orange-tabby-cat-relaxing-in-natural-light-a-moment-of-simple-joy-and.jpg "Gentle Orange Tabby Cat Relaxing in Natural Light a Moment of Simple Joy and Sustainable Outdoor Lifestyle")

### Philosophical Perspectives on Animal Welfare

| Philosopher | Perspective | Implications for Technology |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Peter Singer | Utilitarianism | Use technology to minimize suffering |
| Tom Regan | Animal Rights | Respect inherent value, avoid manipulation |
| Biosemiotics | Sensory Experience | Enhance natural environments, avoid sensory overload |

![A wild fox sits attentively in dry grassland, a testament to sustainable living and environmental awareness. The focused animal, with its earthy brown and grey coat blending with the natural habitat, embodies responsible stewardship of wildlife conservation. Its alert gaze and poised posture highlight the importance of ecological balance within its ecosystem.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/alert-wild-fox-sitting-in-natural-grassland-habitat-exemplifying-sustainable.jpg "Alert Wild Fox Sitting in Natural Grassland Habitat Exemplifying Sustainable Lifestyle and Environmental Awareness")

### Ethical Considerations for Technology in Animal Agriculture

--- SOURCE 4: Animal Welfare: Digital and Data Technologies in Livestock ... ---
URL: https://digi4live.eu/animal-welfare-digital-and-data-technologies/

SUMMARY:
Wearable Devices: Wearable technology has revolutionised animal monitoring. Devices that measure vital signs, activity levels, and other

FULL CONTENT:
![](https://digi4live.eu/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/digi4live-logo-white-horisontal.png)

# Animal Welfare: Digital and Data Technologies in Livestock Farming

![Animal Welfare](https://digi4live.eu/wp-content/uploads/2024/06/enhancing-animal-welfare-01-1024x538.jpg)

**Animal welfare** has become an important topic of ethical consideration in **modern society**, especially concerning **livestock farming**. 

As the global population continues to grow, the demand for **animal products** has surged, often leading to **intensive farming practices** that can endanger the welfare of animals. Ensuring that animals are treated humanely is a moral obligation and a crucial aspect of **sustainable farming practices.**

## **The Importance of Animal Welfare**

Animal welfare refers to the **well-being of animals** under **human care**. According to [**Sentient Media**](https://sentientmedia.org/what-is-animal-welfare/#:~:text=One%20example%20of%20animal%20welfare,are%20aspects%20of%20good%20welfare.), it involves various aspects, including proper housing, nutrition, disease prevention and treatment, responsible care, humane handling, and, when necessary, humane euthanasia. The **fundamental principle of animal welfare** is to ensure that animals do not suffer unnecessarily and live in conditions that allow them to express their natural behaviours.

One example of animal welfare in practice is providing livestock with adequate space, clean water, nutritious food, and proper medical care. These are **aspects of good welfare** that help ensure animals are **healthy and comfortable**. There are several reasons why animal welfare is important:

![Animal Welfare](https://digi4live.eu/wp-content/uploads/2024/06/Animal-welfer-01-1024x538.jpg)

## **Challenges in Animal Welfare**

Despite its importance, maintaining **high standards** of animal welfare in livestock farming presents **several challenges**:

## **Innovations in Animal Welfare: Addressing the Challenges**

Given the challenges, **innovation** and **technology** play a crucial role in **advancing animal welfare**. Here are some notable developments:

## **Precision Livestock Farming: Enhancing Welfare and Safety**

According to a study published in [**Frontiers in Animal Science**](https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fanim.2021.736536/full), **precision livestock farming (PLF) technologies** are integral to improving **animal welfare** and ensuring **food safety**. PLF employs **advanced technologies** like **sensors**, **data analytics**, and **automation** to **monitor** and **manage** the health and well-being of **livestock in real-time**. This approach aligns seamlessly with the objectives of [**Digi4Live**](https://digi4live.eu/).

**Real-Time Monitoring and Early Intervention**: PLF technologies enable continuous monitoring of animal health and behaviour. Sensors can detect early signs of illness, stress, or discomfort, allowing farmers to intervene promptly. This reduces the likelihood of disease outbreaks and enhances overall animal welfare.

**Data-Driven Decision Making**: The data collected through PLF systems provide valuable insights that can inform better decision-making. For example, analysing patterns in feeding behaviour or movement can help optimise feeding schedules and improve living conditions.

**Resource Efficiency and Sustainability**: By optimising the use of resources such as feed, water, and energy, PLF technologies contribute to more sustainable farming practices. This not only benefits the environment but also supports the economic viability of farms.

**Improving Public Health and Food Safety**: Ensuring the health and welfare of livestock is directly linked to food safety. Healthy animals are less likely to harbour pathogens that can be transmitted to humans through animal products. PLF systems help maintain high health standards, thereby protecting public health.

![Animal Welfare](https://digi4live.eu/wp-content/uploads/2024/06/Templete-for-Digi4Live-pictures-01-1024x538.jpg)

## **The Digital Revolution in Animal Health**

The digital revolution in animal health is **transforming** how **farmers and veterinarians care for livestock**. As highlighted by [**Health for Animals**](https://healthforanimals.org/pages/digital-revolution-in-animal-health/), advancements in **digital technologies** are driving significant improvements in **animal health management**. Digital tools such as **mobile apps, cloud-based platforms, and advanced analytics** enable farmers to track and manage animal health more effectively.

**Mobile Apps for Health Tracking**: Mobile applications allow farmers to log health events, treatments, and observations quickly. These apps can provide reminders for vaccinations, monitor health trends, and alert farmers to potential issues before they become severe.

**Cloud-Based Platforms**: Cloud technology facilitates the storage and analysis of vast amounts of health data. Farmers and veterinarians can access health records from anywhere, ensuring timely interventions and better coordination of care.

**Advanced Analytics**: By analysing health data, advanced analytics can identify patterns and predict health issues. Predictive analytics help in early diagnosis and treatment, reducing the risk of disease outbreaks and improving overall animal health.

## **Digi4Live’s Commitment to Animal Welfare**

**[Digi4Live](https://digi4live.eu/)** represents a **significant step forward** in the effort to **improve animal welfare** in livestock farming. By harnessing the **power of digital technology and data**, this project offers a scalable and sustainable solution to the **challenges faced** by the **industry**. The **integration** of **existing data systems** and **adherence** to **good practices** ensure that the welfare of animals is prioritised **without imposing additional burdens** on **farmers** or the **animals themselves**.

By supporting Digi4Live, stakeholders across the agricultural sector can **contribute** to a more ethical and **sustainable future for animal farming**, **benefiting animals**, **farmers**, **consumers**, and the **environment** alike. Digi4Live not only promises to enhance the welfare of livestock but also to pave the way for a more connected and responsive agricultural system in Europe.

enriched_points.point_title: Importance of technology in animal welfare
enriched_points.expanded_content: Technological advancements, such as precision livestock farming and monitoring sensors, enhance animal health, optimize living conditions, and facilitate early disease detection, significantly improving overall welfare.

While technology improves animal care, it raises ethical dilemmas, including reliance on automated systems, potential stress from learning technologies, and intensification risks compromising welfare.

Emerging technologies, like AI and drones, offer transformative opportunities for individualized care, enabling real-time monitoring and proactive management, ultimately fostering sustainable and humane farming practices.
